data.title: Why GPIX Stands Out For Its Capture Ratios Among Similar ETFs
data.description:
Covered call funds are popular for a reason. They monetize volatility for higher yield at the cost of upside cap. But, high yield tells only part of the story. Without sustainable growth of capital base (net asset value or NAV per share), yield can stagnate.

Enter capture ratios. Capture ratios can tell a lot about how covered call funds capture upside/downside relative to their benchmark with no covered call overlay. They are a direct result of a fund’s covered call strategy and can signal the fund’s future returns. If capture ratios are analyzed right, investors can steer clear of ETFs whose distributions and returns are likely to lag behind peers.

Among the funds I compare today, Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF (GPIX) stands out for its most favorable capture ratios. This bodes very well for its long-term performance.

GPIX ETF Explained

GPIX holds stocks in the S&P 500. The fund's target yield is about 8.50%, which is not too far from today's 8%. Depending on market conditions, especially volatility, the current yield can deviate from the 8.50% target. The S&P 500 does not have a high yield at around 1.05%. So, the bulk of GPIX yield comes from selling call options on the S&P 500.

Most covered call funds, especially the older one, use a somewhat inflexible covered call strategy. It does not vary much with market conditions. Conversely, GPIX does vary its portfolio coverage in a systematic way with the target yield in mind. I think Goldman Sachs designed GPIX with the goal of minimizing net asset value, or NAV erosion.

GPIX's focus is to sell at-the-money call options. At-the-money call options come with higher premium income than the out-of-the-money ones. But, they cap upside more when the S&P 500 makes bullish moves.

To counter this negative, Goldman Sachs came up with the "dynamic" portfolio coverage. GPIX does not sell call options to cover a fixed portion of its portfolio. Instead, that coverage varies from 25% to 75%. This percentage is driven by the volatility of the underlying index and how much yield GPIX needs to achieve in a given period.

The main benefit of this approach is that it does not cap GPIX's upside by a lot. This allows the fund to rally with the S&P 500 with very strong upside capture ratios. So, let's define and compare capture ratios of GPIX with other popular ETFs with the S&P 500 as their benchmark.

Capture Ratios Explained

Capture ratios can convey a lot about the risk profile of a covered call fund. The formulas are straightforward:

The upside capture ratio looks at a fund’s returns on up days/months relative to the benchmark. It is the opposite for the downside capture. Ideally, you want a fund with an upside capture as high as possible and downside capture as low as possible.

Investors buy covered call funds with the hope of continuous bull markets. Thus, the worst outcome is when upside capture is below the downside capture. In other words, a fund takes all the beating in bear markets, but is very slow to recover in later rallies.

Let’s consider a hypothetical example. Suppose the S&P 500 goes down by 10% in year 1 and then rallies by 25% in year 2. We have two funds to compare with different capture ratios. Fund 1 and Fund 2 are similar in a sense that their capture ratios are equal. But, fund 2 ratios are lower.

Hypothetical Upside/Downside Capture Example

S&P 500 Fund 1 Fund 2
Base $100.00 $100.00 $100.00
Downside Capture 100% 85% 65%
Upside Capture 100% 85% 65%
Year 1 -10.0% -8.5% -6.5%
Year 2 25.0% 21.3% 16.3%
Balance Year 1 $90.0 $91.5 $93.5
Balance Year 2 $112.5 $110.9 $108.7

We can see that fund 2 is the most defensive one, with a downside capture of only 65%. But when markets rally, fund 2 will be a laggard. Fund 1 is more aggressive and captures a lot of downside. But it will capture a lot of upside too. In bull markets, fund 1 is a clear winner. As it captures more up-moves of its benchmark, fund 1 will go up more.

GPIX vs. SPYI vs. JEPI vs. XYLD

Let’s take a look at four popular covered call ETFs based on the S&P 500. They are:

- Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF (GPIX)

- JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI)

- NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI)

- Global X S&P 500 Covered Call ETF (XYLD)

Note that JEPI does not hold stocks as in the S&P 500 one-for-one. Instead, it selects securities to minimize the volatility of its portfolio. I compiled the upside/downside capture ratios for all funds either from their inception or 10-year time horizon, whichever is available. Here is the data for them with the S&P 500 as their benchmark.

The goal for these funds is to be below the 45% line. In other words, you want to hold a fund whose upside capture is above its downside capture. Anything close to the 45% line is fine, but will appeal to different risk profiles.

GPIX ETF is the most aggressive one with the highest upside/downside capture ratios. This is thanks to that variable portfolio coverage (overwrite levels) I talked about.

Conversely, JEPI takes the most defensive stance. JEPI's goal is to minimize volatility relative to the S&P 500. It won't go down by as much, but it won't go up by as much either. JEPI is an older generation of covered call funds that goes heavy on writing covered calls through so-called equity-linked notes, or ELNs. In up-trending markets, JEPI will lag behind its peers and may be subject to material NAV erosion risk.

Conversely, we have SPYI as a middle-ground covered call ETF. SPYI prioritizes higher yield and is more aggressive when writing call options. But, it takes an active approach. SPYI manages its portfolio in such a way that it has a robust upside capture ratio of ~74%.

Finally, we have XYLD. The fund writes at-the-money call options. It does not use variable portfolio coverage like what GPIX does. This inflexible structure produces an unattractive capture profile. Its upside capture is below its downside capture. All of this produced subpar long-term results. Very often, XYLD struggles with recovery from down episodes and exhibits signs of NAV erosion.

How Capture Ratios Translate Into Total Returns

Let's see total returns and volatility-adjusted metrics for these funds and observe how they relate to upside/downside capture ratios.

Return Metrics for Nov 2023 - Dec 2025

Metric JEPI GPIX SPYI XYLD SPDR S&P 500 ETF
Start Balance $10,000.00 $10,000.00 $10,000.00 $10,000.00 $10,000.00
End Balance $12,995.00 $15,845.00 $14,887.00 $13,519.00 $16,776.00
Annualized Return 12.85% 23.67% 20.16% 14.93% 26.97%
Standard Deviation 7.49% 9.77% 8.04% 6.19% 11.52%
Maximum Drawdown -5.67% -6.81% -5.51% -7.01% -7.58%
Sharpe Ratio 1.04 1.77 1.76 1.54 1.75
Upside Capture 53% 86% 73% 52% 100%
Downside Capture 78% 85% 74% 46% 100%
Current Yield 7.40% 8.04% 11.61% 10.45% 1.06%

Source: Portfolio Visualizer

Focusing on yield alone would steer an investor to either XYLD or SPYI. However, high yield alone may produce subpar total returns if looked at in isolation. In this sense, upside/downside capture ratios help gain insights into how different covered call strategies translate into total returns.

Since November 2023, the S&P 500 has almost been on an uninterrupted uptrend. Because of GPIX capturing more upside, it clearly outperformed other funds. SPYI came out as second. It has a very attractive Sharpe Ratio thanks to its lower downside capture. Its heavier use of covered calls lowered its downside and reduced its standard deviation.

JEPI was the biggest laggard due to how it captures the lowest upside from the S&P 500. Surprisingly, XYLD outperformed JEPI. That's despite its less attractive upside vs downside capture in the last 10 years. The culprit for this discrepancy is how these capture ratios may vary from period to period.

As it happened, JEPI had a worse downside capture from November 2023 to December 2025. That explains it. But, I still expect JEPI to beat XYLD on a long-term horizon. JEPI sells slightly out-of-the-money call options. This allows it to take part in future rallies better than XYLD.

Investors Takeaways

Looking at capture ratios can tell a lot about how a fund's covered call strategy translates into its returns. They are largely the results of how a given fund manages its written option positions.

Ideally, an investor would want a high upside and a low downside capture. But, funds like that are rare. Based on today's analysis, most covered call funds have upside and downside capture ratios very close to each other.

SPYI would be best when the uptrend is not very pronounced, with some up and down movements. Because SPYI targets a higher yield compared to its peers, its upside capture is lower than for GPIX. But its active approach is its valuable asset that keeps upside/downside capture in line.

JEPI is probably the third best. It will do well when markets keep falling. Its low downside capture will keep a lid on its drawdowns. But, I don't think this is a sustainable long-term approach. Investors buy covered call ETFs for their long-term appreciation that these funds use to generate yield. With low upside capture, JEPI is very likely to show distributions that either stagnate or grow at low rates compared to SPYI or GPIX.

As for XYLD, its long-term upside capture turned out to be lower than its downside capture. If this relationship persists into the future, XYLD is likely to underperform all the other three funds that I looked at today. Not only will its distributions stagnate or grow slowly, its total returns will be subpar compared to JEPI, SPYI, and GPIX.

Finally, in a long-term uptrend, a higher upside capture should beat a low upside capture, keeping other things constant. We saw how GPIX is doing better thanks to its variable portfolio coverage for covered calls. It manages to trend up well when the S&P 500 goes up. And even though it may go down by more, it will recover much faster and overtake the other funds long-term. GPIX is probably the best-covered fund of the four that I looked at under this scenario.

data.title: Why Factor Selection Is Vital In Systematic Fixed Income
data.description:
A Multifactor Approach in Action

Change in a Bond’s Multifactor Score Triggers a Purchase

Systematic fixed-income investing is an active approach that aims to beat bond market returns by identifying and harnessing the factors that drive bond market performance.

Well-known factors such as value and momentum are widely recognized to have predictive power. Advanced systematic approaches, however, seek to identify additional drivers of performance—including proprietary factors—to integrate into their multifactor models.

Using a specific bond as a case study, the display above shows how a multifactor model determined a total score for the bond based on its underlying factor scores.

At the start of the year, the bond scored highly on well-known factors such as value and quality. However, it scored low on some of the other factors. This reduced the total multifactor score, avoiding a premature purchase.

By the end of February, several of the factors’ scores had improved significantly, leading to a higher total multifactor score and to the bond being added to the portfolio.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Where The Rubber Meets Return: The ESG Factors That Matter
data.description:
By Patrick O'Connell, CFA | John Huang, CFA | Erin Bigley, CFA

The materiality of ESG factors differs across sectors and markets. Investors need to understand how.

As environmental, social and governance (ESG) factors help contribute to—or detract from—security returns, it makes sense for active investors to integrate them into security selection. But there’s a wide disparity in the materiality of ESG factors across investment sectors and markets. In our view, understanding this dynamic is the key to successfully incorporating ESG risks and opportunities into portfolio construction.

For many investors, whether fixed income or equity, the process of integrating ESG factors into their strategies begins with correlating the relevance of each factor to individual industries. At a basic level this shows, for example, that greenhouse gas emissions are a particular risk for mining companies and electric utilities, while customer privacy is a key concern for the healthcare sector.

This is a good starting point but offers an incomplete perspective. We believe a much deeper dive is necessary to fully dimension the materiality of ESG factors for portfolio performance. Investors need to know how a particular factor may affect investment returns for a given sector or market.

Factors Can Have Wide or Narrow Impacts

Factor attribution using historical returns can reveal how ESG factors have contributed to investment returns in the past, whether for a sector or an entire investment universe, in equities or in bonds.

We’ve observed that some factors can be financially material for all companies in a market, regardless of sector. For example, we divided stocks in the MSCI All Country World Index into quintiles according to their total recordable incident rate (TRIR)—the number of workplace injuries or illnesses—then compared their returns relative to the parent index over 14 years (Display, left). The results show that high TRIR consistently underperformed the market and that low TRIR consistently outperformed.

Some ESG Factors Affect Entire Markets

Similarly, in the bond market, “social fines” is a powerful, index-wide factor (Display, right). Social fines are regulatory penalties imposed for nonenvironmental reasons, such as workplace health and safety and anticompetitive practices.

Other ESG factors with broad relevance across investment sectors include CEOs' length of tenure and employee turnover. For investors wishing to integrate ESG factors into their portfolios, it’s useful, in our view, to know which factors have index-wide applicability.

Factor attribution can also reveal which ESG factors are particularly relevant to a specific sector and which have historically shown no financial materiality (Display).

In Other Cases, Factor Relevance Varies by Sector

Water Usage Intensity vs. Annualized Return Relative to the MSCI All Country World Index (Percent)

Another advantage of factor attribution is that it can lead to observations that are unexpected and even counterintuitive. We found, for example, that companies with high ESG disclosures broadly performed better than those with low or no disclosures, regardless of whether their ESG practices were good, bad, or indifferent. In the case of ESG metrics where there was no significant under- or overperformance relative to the market—CFO tenure and split roles for CEO and chair of the board—companies that disclosed data outperformed companies that didn’t disclose, on average.

Fundamental Research Enhances Insights from Factor Attribution

But factor attribution alone is not enough, in our view; it should complement fundamental research.

Understanding the effect of ESG factors on performance is most valuable in the context of broader research into how well a company is managed. For example, fundamental research can show that a high TRIR affects productivity directly, through lost working hours, and indirectly, by creating a culture in which workers are undermotivated because they don’t feel safe. Additionally, factor attribution works best with long data series, which are not always available, stressing the importance of fundamental research.

Another way fundamental research can help is in measuring ESG factors appropriately to a particular sector instead of taking the generic approach typically used by many third-party ESG databases. This could mean, for example, measuring carbon emissions in terms of miles per gallon for automakers, per passenger mile for airliners and per ton of cement produced for building-material companies.

And it can tease out the nuances underlying many ESG factors. In the case of the mining sector, for example, fundamental research can focus on tailings dam risk within the more broadly defined factors of water and hazardous materials management (Display).

Deep and Complex Insights Can Be Captured Quickly and Efficiently

Example of an ESG Factor Map

As this small snapshot of an ESG materiality matrix shows, these insights can be mapped very simply. But it’s the quality of the information behind it that gives the map its value: the understanding of how ESG factors can be financially material across investment sectors, industries and markets. By embedding such knowledge in their securities research and portfolio construction, investors, in our view, may significantly enhance the potential for outperformance.

The authors wish to thank Peter Højsteen-Ljungbeck for his contribution.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations and do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams. Views are subject to revision over time.

MSCI makes no express or implied warranties or representations, and shall have no liability whatsoever with respect to any MSCI data contained herein.

The MSCI data may not be further redistributed or used as a basis for other indices or any securities or financial products. This report is not approved, reviewed or produced by MSCI.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Commodities: Lingering Supply Risks Provide Tailwinds To Oil
data.description:
By Warren Patterson, Head of Commodities Strategy and Ewa Manthey, Commodities Strategist

Oil prices continue to move higher amid supply risks, while heightened geopolitical tensions are driving gold and silver prices to fresh record highs.

Energy: Supply risks linger

Oil prices rallied for a third consecutive day yesterday, with ICE Brent trading close to US$64/bbl. The prompt ICE Brent timespread has also strengthened through January amid growing supply risks.

The price increase comes amid intensifying protests in Iran, raising the possibility of some form of intervention by the US. There have been suggestions of the potential for US military action. Up until now, President Trump has said the US will impose a 25% tariff on any country “doing business” with Iran.

China is a key buyer of Iranian oil. Whether this secondary tariff threat is sufficient to push China away from Iranian oil remains to be seen. Previously, the threat of secondary tariffs on imports of Venezuelan and Russian oil persuaded China to halt purchases. With the US and China having reached a trade truce, we question whether the US would want to rock the boat again with additional tariffs on China.

Exports of Kazakh oil from the CPC terminal will come under significant pressure this month. Shipments are expected to come in between 800-900k b/d, around 45% below initial expectations, according to Bloomberg. The drop is due to maintenance and damage caused by Ukrainian drones, while weather has also been an issue.

European natural gas prices surged yesterday, with TTF settling 6.6% higher, returning to above EUR30/MWh. Colder weather across large parts of Europe increases heating demand, while unrest in Iran is a concern for gas markets.

First, there are potential risks to LNG flows from the Persian Gulf. Second, there’s the potential for disruptions to Iranian gas flows to Turkey. Given the large TTF fund short, it wouldn’t take much to move the market as funds run in to cover shorts.

Metals: Metals power higher

Metals continue to power higher at the start of the week. Tight supplies and robust demand expectations are underpinning a broadly bullish mood across the complex.

In precious metals, gold climbed to fresh records as a range of supportive factors converged. They include geopolitical risks, elevated fiscal uncertainty, strong central bank demand, and ongoing concerns about inflation and monetary credibility.

The US Justice Department threatened the Federal Reserve with a criminal indictment, reviving concerns over the central bank’s independence. The dollar fell, boosting the prices of metals. Protests in Iran keep geopolitical tensions elevated, while President Trump has reiterated threats to take Greenland, bringing further upside to precious metals.

Gold surpassed $4,600/oz in yesterday’s trading, while silver surged past $85/oz. Silver has already climbed around 20% so far this year, following an almost 150% surge last year. This has pushed the gold/silver ratio below 60, the lowest level since 2013

In industrial metals, copper rose toward a record amid ongoing concerns over supply, while the US continues to import record volumes ahead of any potential tariffs. Comex copper inventories reached an all-time high after expanding for 42 straight weeks. But they remain below total warehouse capacity. This is keeping markets outside the US tight.

Aluminium, meanwhile, traded at its highest since 2022. Supply is tight everywhere except Indonesia, as smelters now face greater competition for power from AI data centres, which are willing to pay much higher prices for long-term contracts.

Agriculture: Bearish WASDE report

The USDA’s latest WASDE report was largely bearish, as reflected in grain price action yesterday. The USDA revised its 2025/26 US corn production estimates by 269m bushels to a record 17bn bushels thanks to higher harvest area and yields.

This exceeded the average market expectation of 16.6b bushels. As a result, ending stock estimates were revised up by 198m bushels to 2.2bn bushels. For the global corn balance, 2025/26 ending stock estimates were revised upward from 279.2mt to 290.9mt, primarily due to higher supplies.

The market was expecting a figure closer to 280 mt. Global corn production estimates rose by 13.1mt to 1,296mt primarily due to increased supplies from the US (+6.8mt) and China (+6.2mt).

The outlook for US soybeans in 2025/26 shows higher supplies, demand, and ending stocks. The USDA revised up US soybean supplies by 9m bushels to 4.3bn bushels.

Stronger supply increased 2025/26 ending stocks by 60m bushels to 350m bushels. For the global soybean balance, 2025/26 ending stock projections were revised upward from 122.4 mt to 124.4 mt, primarily due to higher supplies.

For the U.S. wheat market, the agency increased its ending stocks estimate from 901m bushels to 926m bushels (+8% year on year) due to lower consumption and higher beginning stocks. For the global wheat market, the USDA raised production estimates to 842.2mt from 837.8mt. As a result, 2025/26 ending stock estimates were increased from 274.9mt to 278.3mt at the end of 2025/26.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: U.S. REIT M&A Activity Ramps Up In H2 2025
data.description:
Real estate M&A activity involving US publicly traded equity real estate investment trusts picked up in the second half of 2025, with six announced deals totaling $16.28 billion in transaction value. By comparison, there were only two deals in the first half of 2025, with a combined transaction value of $1.72 billion, according to an S&P Global Market Intelligence analysis.

The analysis included real estate deals where either the buyer or target is an equity REIT listed on the Nasdaq, NYSE or NYSE American.

2025 deals

Mortgage REIT Rithm Capital Corp.'s (RITM) acquisition of office REIT Paramount Group Inc. was 2025's largest equity REIT M&A transaction, with a deal value of $5.77 billion, including debt assumption. Under the terms of the deal, announced in September 2025, Rithm acquired all outstanding common shares of Paramount Group for $6.60 per share, totaling approximately $1.46 billion, and about $4.31 billion in assumed debt. The acquisition was completed in December.

Another office REIT acquisition in 2025 was announced in July, when City Office REIT Inc. (CIO) agreed to be taken private by an affiliate of Elliott Investment Management LP and Morning Calm Management LLC. The transaction is valued at $1.10 billion, comprising approximately $293.1 million in cash and $746.6 million in assumed debt.

Three other REIT privatizations were announced in the fourth quarter of 2025.

In October 2025, industrial REIT Plymouth Industrial REIT Inc. (PLYM) agreed to be acquired in a $2.10 billion transaction by entities affiliated with Makarora Management LP and Ares Management Corp. Sotherly Hotels Inc. (SOHO), meanwhile, agreed to be taken private in a $516.4 million transaction by a joint venture backed by Kemmons Wilson Hospitality Partners LP and Ascendant Capital Partners LP.

In December 2025, Hawaii-focused REIT Alexander & Baldwin Inc. (ALEX) announced an agreement to be taken private in a $2.30 billion transaction by a joint venture formed by MW Group Ltd. and funds affiliated with Blackstone Inc. (BX) and Divco West Real Estate Services LLC.

Additionally, in October 2025, timber REITs Rayonier Inc. (RYN) and PotlatchDeltic Corp. (PCH) announced their agreement to merge in transaction valued at $4.49 billion.

In the first half of 2025, two healthcare REIT deals were disclosed.

Welltower Inc. (WELL) announced in January 2025 that it agreed to acquire nontraded REIT NorthStar Healthcare Income Inc. (NHHS) in an all-cash deal with an approximate enterprise value of about $900 million. CareTrust REIT Inc. (CTRE) disclosed in March 2025 that it agreed to acquire UK-based Care REIT PLC for approximately $817 million after accounting for $240 million of assumed net debt.

The total value of transactions where public REITs acquired other public REITs reached approximately $11.1 billion in 2025. Meanwhile, deals where public REITs were taken private amounted to about $6.0 billion.

While most REITs traded at discounts to their respective net asset value estimates as of year-end 2025, the market-cap-weighted Dow Jones Equity All REIT index closed the year at a 0.2% premium to net asset value.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ValuEngine Weekly Market Summary And Commentary
data.description:
U.S. equity markets continued their constructive start to 2026, with broad participation across styles, sectors, and market capitalizations. While mega-cap technology continues to attract attention, recent performance data reinforces a theme we have been highlighting for several months - rotation toward value, cyclicals, and smaller capitalization stocks remains intact.

In the below tables we use major ETFs as a proxy for some major indexes as well as each of the sector groups into which we divide the overall markets. Tracking these over time provides a more defined picture of the US markets than simply tracking major indexes. This is followed by notable individual stock movers over the past month, before presenting our full strategy outlook.

Strategy Note

Using State Street’s SPDR ETF universe as a microcosmic subset to take a quick look at year-to-date 2026 performance, the top performing mini-sector index ETFs thus far are Aerospace (XAR) and Metals & Mining (XME) at 14.3% and 12.9% respectively. XME gets our highest rating of 5 (Strong Buy). Meanwhile, Pharmaceuticals (XPH) and Utilities (XLU) are off to the lowest starts with price declines of about 0.4% thus far.

The rotation themes we noted as prevalent in the fourth quarter of 2025 are still present at the start of 2026. Although the S&P 500 Portfolio ETF (SPYM) is up 1.8%, the State Street SPDR S&P 500 Value ETF (SPYV) is up 2.5%, while growth counterpart SPYG has risen just 1.1%.

The differences in market cap size are even more dramatic. State Street SPDR S&P 400 Mid Cap Portfolio ETF (SPMD) is up 4.7%, while State Street SPDR S&P 600 Small Cap Portfolio ETF (SPSM) gained 5.1%. Size matters, but in the past four months it has swung back to the smaller the better.

The relative underperformance in the first six days of the trading year notwithstanding, ValuEngine’s ratings are unperturbed. The Technology and Health Care State Street Select Sector SPDR ETFs, XLK and XLV, respectively, still get our highest rating of 5.

Among all the 2025 outlooks we perused this week, we are particularly struck by the bearish persistence of investment legend Jeremy Grantham, co-founder of Boston-based Grantham, Mayo and Van Otterloo. While reading a recent Barron’s interview, one quote took his pessimism to new extremes: “I would recommend zero exposure to the US, but if you have to own US stocks, own quality stocks.”

In GMO parlance, these are defined as companies with high, stable returns, consistent earnings and dividend growth, low debt, and a degree of monopoly power or price control. These are the factor guidelines in the stated objective of Grantham Mayo’s active ETF QLTY.

QLTY and its holdings fit the same profile as three much larger U.S. equity quality factor ETFs: iShares MSCI USA Quality Factor ETF (QUAL), Invesco S&P 500 Quality ETF (SPHQ), and Fidelity Quality Factor ETF (FQAL). All are rated 3 (Hold) at the present time by ValuEngine.

To look at individual stocks in QLTY. We used ETFdb, a TMX VettaFi product, as the source for QLTY’s holdings and found two stocks rated 5 (Strong Buy): Broadcom (AVGO) and Eli Lilly and Company (LLY).

Ten additional holdings are rated 4 (Buy), including Lam Research (LRCX), Alphabet (GOOGL), and KLA Corporation (KLAC). Having roughly one-third of its holdings rated 4 or 5 approximately doubles the percentage of recommended stocks in our broader universe, positioning QLTY for potentially superior performance in the months ahead.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Weyerhauser: 'Hold' 4 Years Ago Was The Right Thing - Moving Forward
data.description:
Some companies/stocks move in very long, long cycles. Weyerhaeuser (WY) is one such company. If you follow my work at all, you will know that I'm a prolific investor and writer on the lumber sector as well as its ancillaries, paper, and packaging. My articles on these sectors and sub-sectors go back many years here on Seeking Alpha. My current, by far, largest investment is in Finnish lumber and industrial company UPM-Kymmene (UPMMY).

What these investments have in common is that the underlying value and industrial cycles in timber and lumber are very long-term. Years ago when I started investing and studying the trends deeper, more than just annual and quarterly reports, I noted trends such as growth in engineered wooden products and materials (something we've been using in Scandinavia for a very long time), as well as secular decline in demand for things like legacy printing paper, instead focusing on things like packaging and products in the hygiene/care sector.

These trend shifts in turn necessitated significant CapEx to either refurbish or build plants/assets that worked with that, replacing much of the legacy plants and machinery that was built to serve the printing paper industry. Europe is the leader in sawn timber manufacturing and using wood for building, while emerging markets, as long as they are not too far south (south/equator usually means building in stone more than in wood), lead demand increases. Secondarily to this, we've also come to see entrants and companies like UPM focusing on things like biofuels and green diesel - something else I believe is going to be on the rise in the future.

Since we're looking at Weyerhaeuser, we're looking at the American timber industry. Trends here are mostly correlated to a very volatile underlying housing market, rising competition for timberland (despite its size, the sourcing in the US is more limited than in Europe), and trade disputes with Canada have added to the uncertainty in the area. Given these dynamics, the current situation in US timber is an oversupply, which is pressuring smaller businesses and adding to volatility.

A bit of a warning, this article contains some terms of art for the timber industry, and some fairly intricate valuation considerations, based on uncertain assumptions for the market, more so than other articles. I am comfortable in this, because I spend a lot of time in timber investments, but realize that this company is neither short-term stable nor conservative as an income investment.

My neutral view has been correct

In my initial article, which you can find here, I took a neutral view on Weyerhaeuser, which has proven correct. Whether this was part of the general underperformance of certain REIT sectors, or a part of the general trend in the lumber industry, is hard to tell - both sectors have had their challenges since that article.

This company, as it was in 2021, is one of the US's leading timberland and forest companies, and one of the largest owners of forests in the entire world (private ones, that is - states in some cases own a lot more). The company has vertical integration in its operation/business model. What this means is that it manages, harvests, and plants new trees - but also works on turning those trees into materials, such as timber, OSB, or other engineered materials. Given that it also owns a lot of property, it can secondarily leverage that property much like landowners are doing in Scandinavia as well - meaning they are using it for wind power plants, gas rights, oil rights, mineral rights, and carbon-capturing solutions.

On the surface level, this company, organized in the form of a REIT, at the very least sounds attractive (and in my view also is, at the right price). This is especially when you consider that WY, in one form or another, has been around for well over 125 years.

The problem or issue with the investment is obvious. If indeed there is a strong track record, and indeed there has been performance acceleration (from some perspective, there has), then why is the company trading at what is a 5-year low or close to it? Is it really just volatile end markets, or are there "sleeping issues" in the stock that the market, or someone here, is seeing?

I would argue that the primary reason the stock is down so much is the strong correlation to housing and new housing construction, which is low. This, in turn, leads to a muted demand for all of the company's products. The mortgage rates, which in the US are high, are not helping. I view it as very unlikely that the company will see any sort of significant turnaround until housing market fundamentals actually improve.

Once these fundamentals improve, then the value will likely go higher, for several reasons. Like UPM, WY's size and vertical integration mean that its operational profit is limited only by the costs associated with operating and the market demand and pricing. While WY doesn't have southern-American operations like UPM (opening up lower cost markets), there's still low cost potential here. It aligns logs to mills, providing operational proximity and stability, and despite the value here, I want to make it clear that WY provides a strong ongoing cash flow.

In many ways, it's obviously companies like this that we should be focusing on. Deeply discounted companies with upside. Unfortunately, this company does not present well when analyzing or trying to value it (more on that later). It also provides what I would consider uncertain guidance and forecasts, because more than 50-70% of the time on a 1-year to 2-year basis, the company misses hitting its targets even with a 10-20% margin of error. What this means, coupled with the underlying uncertainty of the end markets, is that I believe guidance or forecasts here cannot be trusted.

Regardless of the company's fundamentals and assets, it does not control, for lack of a better term, its own destiny. Its geography means that it's at the mercy of a small variety of end markets, and these end markets have been declining significantly since 2022, also the reason why things have gone down for so long.

The company is BBB rated, has a somewhat decent yield of about 3%, but as you'll see, it's not currently covered by AEPS (more on the metric below). Its historical inaccuracy means that I completely understand, and did 4 years ago, why investing in it is tricky and why I prefer investing in European-based timber companies that enjoy different trends and fundamentals. European timber markets enjoy a higher price stability thanks to certifications and EU regulations like EUTR, which closes the market to some extent to illegal timber. Many countries have their own policies that enhance this. It makes the EU operating market a more stable one than the US, despite what, from a business perspective, is a technically more attractive market due to lower regulation. However, this also comes to bite WY and other companies in the behind, because it opens the market up to low-cost, illegally sourced, and cut timbers at low prices.

Put another way, I demand a significant, cheap valuation to invest here.

And unfortunately, it has nothing to do with the company's fundamentals, which are solid, nor its ability to hit goals, which it has.

It simply has to do with the fact that the US market is, by some investors in timber and forest, considered uninvestable due to the inherent challenges, which come in addition to typical challenges with timber investment.

Valuation for Weyerhaeuser

The reasons I'm providing this update are several. First, I believe in a turnaround in timber (eventually). We're already seeing signs in Europe, and while US market dynamics are likely to slow it, I believe a reversal and peak is coming. Secondly, WY currently trades at a deeply depressed discount. It's come to a point where I believe that the company is trading below the estimated value of its assets, meaning the timberland it owns, as well as the potential long-term cash flow from that.

And that is attractive, because it's the reason I've invested so much in UPM. Unfortunately, WY does not have the safety, the yield, the credit rating, or the near-term upside clarity of UPM. Therefore, it needs to be discounted.

Typically, when it comes to REITs, I apply an AFFO as the metric - it's a true measure of cash generation, because it adjusts for recurring capex and leasing costs. However, because of what Weyerhaeuser is and does, I don't believe AFFO is the best metric for the company, because the timber industry significantly differs from standard REITs, making AFFO or similar REIT-oriented KPIs less relevant.

I use either EBITDA or AEPS as the metric here. Unfortunately, in terms of AEPS, it doesn't give us much to hang onto here either. The company is trading at an average weighted P/E of 148x, to an average of 35x P/E, with 10 out of the last 20 years at negative AEPS growth, and then the next 2-3 years at over 100% average EPS growth on average, with 2027E at about 245%.

Simply put, there's a general expectation of a reversal, but the company's forecast uncertainty makes it unclear when it might come.

I therefore believe the best approach to valuing the company is to take the net asset value of its timberlands, apply a discount rate, and then split that by shares outstanding. The discount rate for the company has typically been about 10-15%, so given my own demands and conservative approach, I'd double that - put it at 30%, maybe 35%. The challenge then remains to put a price on the company's extensive timberland reserves. On a book value perspective, the company has a current equity or book value at about $9.7B for fiscal 2024, the last year we have, corresponding to about $13/share. The problem is that these assets (timber) are carried at historical cost on the balance sheet, which means that the value is (likely) significantly higher. At least, it is in Europe.

Even if I value the company at significantly below current transaction prices for timberland in the US, about $2,700-$2,800/acre in 2024-2025, and go no higher than $1,800, this still comes to a NAV of about $35/share. Some analysts price WY at $38/share, and some go as low as $26/share. I would discount my own discount more and put the company at a fair value of about $32/share, with a PT of $27/share. This comes to an almost 25% discount to my already discounted NAV, and makes the company a "BUY".

The company being a "BUY" isn't necessarily enough to cause me to buy here. I also compare it to European timber businesses, which, as I mentioned above, are fundamentally more attractive to me.

However, at this time, I believe valuation dictates that WY be a "BUY" with a PT of $27/share.

Risks & Considerations

As an American timber REIT/company, the business's primary exposure lies with the housing market, the primary consumer of its materials, and because it's much more homogenous than in Europe, also more risky. It opens WY up to the risk of economic recession, and the volatility in housing has shown, in the share and sales price, just how bad such a downturn can be.

Tariffs and trade policies are the second risk, as the company also exports many of its products. The current state of oversupply means that there's intense competition, and WY isn't necessarily the biggest competitor or the best one out there. Pricing power for the company right now is low, expressed in the valuation, and inventories are likely to remain high for the time being.

Intense costs for homebuilding, as well as a potentially rising mortgage rate, pose further risks to the company, and I wouldn't necessarily add to my risk profile by investing here, unless doing so is done at a very good price.

There's also the ancillary risk that comes with operating legacy assets - best exemplified by Weyerhaeuser's asbestos litigation, which has been following the company around for some time. In other geographies where laws are not as punitive towards companies in terms of costs, such as Sweden, this would be less of a risk - but in the US, given the legal situation, it's one that's at least worth mentioning.

These are the primary risks to the company as it stands.

Thesis

Weyerhaeuser is a storied timber REIT with significant assets in qualitative timber. I believe it's a question of time until we see outperformance or good performance on the part of the company. I like what I see in the valuation here but also believe that the risk is significant, for all the reasons mentioned above.

I believe that you may, and are likely to, outperform the market over time if you buy here, though your timeframe will have to be longer than I might have originally expected. My price target is $27/share, updated for 2026. That being said, the company is incredibly volatile and exposed to end markets with a lot of uncertainty. Not taking this into consideration means that you're likely to, in the short term, be disappointed by the company.

Keep this in mind prior to deciding whether to buy or not.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which typically is enough. Based on this, and a price target of $27/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of January 2026.

data.title: President Trump's QE Plans Could Boost Housing And The Stock Market
data.description:
As we all know, President Trump has said that Fed Chair Jerome Powell is "too late" when it comes to interest rate cuts, and he has even talked about firing him. Jerome Powell's term ends in May, and President Trump is expected to appoint a new Fed Chair soon, but it seems that he can't wait that long to start lowering rates as well as come up with stimulus for the U.S. economy. There's a good reason for President Trump to want to boost the economy as well as the stock market as soon as possible, and that is because the midterm elections are coming up later this year.

Midterm elections are typically not favorable for the party that holds the White House, and with the job market showing signs of deterioration over the past few months, things could get worse in the coming months if nothing is done to stimulate the economy soon. In addition, in the recent high-profile New York City Mayor race, a socialist Democrat who spoke to voters about affordability came out on top in the election. All of this suggests that President Trump needs to take action since the Federal Reserve has not been able to significantly reduce mortgage rates, and he also needs to work to make life more affordable for voters.

The latest news is that Jerome Powell is being criminally investigated by the Department of Justice for his testimony regarding renovations at the historic Federal Reserve building. I view this as a concern because it is unprecedented, and it could lead to volatility in the financial markets. However, the market seems to be shrugging off any concerns for now, which shows resilience, but this still could have consequences for the markets later on. This move gives the potential appearance that the Fed will be less independent under Trump and that Jerome Powell's replacement could be viewed as President Trump's right-hand man at the Fed. The bond market could revolt against this if it decides that inflation is being swept aside by the Fed in the future.

Let's take a look at some challenges and at how President Trump is taking action to stimulate the economy and lower the cost of living, because if his plans work, it might be just what is needed to stabilize the job market, boost growth, and even improve consumer confidence before the all-important midterm elections. Let's start with some of the economic and stock market challenges first and then go into what Trump is doing to create his own version of QE and other stimulus:

Trump's QE Plan Could Be the Catalyst This Market Needs

The S&P 500 Index (SPY) is near record highs, which is right around the 7,000 level. This is a round number, and it seems to be a resistance level since it traded close to this level in October and December, as well as right now. If you look at the chart below, you can see that this index appears to be having difficulty in making a decisive breakthrough past the 7,000 level. If a breakout past this level keeps getting rejected, it will make a multiple top pattern more clear on the chart, and that could lead to a breakdown in this index rather than a breakthrough. However, if President Trump continues to roll out ideas that are intended to boost the economy, it could be just what is needed to create a decisive upward move for stocks.

Consumer Confidence is Extremely Weak

The Consumer Confidence Index has been generally trending lower for the past few years, and it is now approaching a level not seen since the lows of the pandemic. I view this as a warning sign for the economy and the stock market, since sustained and significant declines in this index are typical during times of recession. This can be seen on the chart below during the Great Financial Crisis and the pandemic. The Consumer Confidence Index dropped by 3.8 points in December amid apparent concerns over the job market and household income.

Housing Unaffordable for Many Americans

In December I wrote an article suggesting that President Trump plans to make housing more affordable in 2026 and that investors should consider buying beaten-down stocks that are related to housing. This included stocks like Whirlpool (WHR), RE/MAX (RMAX), Lennar (LEN), Home Depot (HD), and even RH (RH). All of these stocks are up significantly in the past couple of weeks, but there could be more gains ahead if President Trump unleashes a sustained rebound in the housing market, which has been frozen for years. But, as shown in the chart below, housing prices are at bubble-like levels, and while President Trump's recent initiatives can ease some of the affordability issues, more needs to be done to make a dent in the high price of home ownership.

Trump's QE Plan

QE, or Quantitative Easing, is a monetary policy that is typically undertaken by central banks in order to stimulate the economy. It typically works by having the central bank buy financial assets such as government bonds in order to inject money into the economy and lower interest rates. QE is typically used when traditional interest rate cuts are not enough to get the economy moving.

On January 8th, President Trump announced that he would ask his representatives to buy $200 billion worth of mortgage bonds in an attempt to lower mortgage rates. Not all the details are clear on this plan yet, but the market moved on this news, and mortgage rates dropped immediately from about 6.2% down to around 6%. It remains to be seen as to whether this reduction in mortgage rates will be sustained and if the mortgage bond buying plan will even happen.

Boosting Consumer Sentiment, Economy

There doesn't seem to be any quick fix or singular solution to boosting consumer confidence or in making housing affordable again, so it seems that President Trump is taking a multi-pronged approach. One of the biggest potential positives could be his recent initiatives in Venezuela, which used to be a country that produced much more oil. President Trump is pushing for American oil companies to go back into Venezuela and get production back up, and he has a stated goal to push the price of oil down to around $50 per barrel. Lower oil prices could reduce inflation in many areas, and that could ease bond market concerns over inflation and allow for more interest rate cuts.

President Trump recently announced a proposal to ban institutional investors from buying single-family homes. I think this is a great idea that will resonate with many voters and possibly increase the amount of homes that are on the market for families to buy instead of corporations. President Trump is planning to announce more details on this and other potential housing affordability plans when he speaks at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, which is scheduled to happen later this month.

Some of the other initiatives by President Trump to boost the economy, housing, and consumer confidence include a proposal for 50-year mortgages and a 10% cap on the interest rate charged by credit card companies. I don't view these proposals as being a good solution because a 50-year mortgage will also carry a higher interest rate, thereby offsetting some of the benefits of a lower (for longer) payment.

While the 10% cap on credit card interest rates could be very popular with voters in the short term, I believe this could backfire and actually cause an economic contraction because the banks could simply decide not to maintain credit card accounts with lower-quality borrowers without a commensurate reward for the risk. If banks respond by closing accounts, this could cut off a key source of consumer spending, which is critical to the U.S. economy.

Risk Factors to Consider

While I am not investing in the major market indexes due to high concentration risks and what I see as overvaluation, I do see value in beaten-down housing-related stocks, so I am buying some stocks in this sector. However, it remains to be seen if many of President Trump's affordability plans will even happen and if they will be successful in the long term. While the Trump "QE" plan to lower mortgage rates did create an immediate decline in mortgage rates, this could just be a short-term move that is unsustainable.

I am concerned about a potential recession that could develop if consumer confidence and the job market do not stabilize soon. If a recession ensues, the stock market could plunge from record high levels, and the housing stocks I have been buying will also likely see major declines. With unconventional and possibly untested ideas, there is also a chance for policy errors, which I believe could backfire, such as trying to cap credit card interest rates.

In Summary

The housing market has been frozen for a few years due to the big increase in mortgage rates that has occurred since the pandemic. Long-term interest rates have not been very responsive to recent Fed rate cuts, and this has kept mortgage rates elevated. However, President Trump appears prepared to do what it takes to reduce mortgage rates by potentially creating his own QE, as well as other affordability initiatives. If he succeeds in unfreezing the housing market, he could unleash a powerful turnaround in this beaten-down sector and provide the economy with a major boost.

data.title: Deutsche Bank: From Rerating To Proof-Of-Execution
data.description:
Introduction

Deutsche Bank Aktiengesellschaft's (DB) shares trading above book value for the first time since 2008 marks a genuine inflection point, but not for the reason equity bulls might hope. This is not the start of a new growth phase but rather the formal end of the recovery trade. After more than a decade in which investors priced in litigation risk, capital fragility, and latent balance-sheet problems, the market is now prepared to accept Deutsche Bank’s reported equity at face value. That trust reset is relevant, but what follows from it is more constrained.

For years, Deutsche Bank traded at a discount because survival itself was questioned. Legacy legal settlements, repeated restructurings, and the corrosive effect of negative interest rates in Europe left earnings volatile and confidence fragile. When the stock briefly touched 0.2x book during the early stages of the pandemic, the market was effectively pricing in a scenario where equity could be impaired again. That period is now over, as shares have risen above the bank’s most recently reported book value. That milestone reflects the cumulative effect of resolved legal disputes, exits from loss-making activities, and a more stable earnings base.

Why the Tier 2 deal matters more than the share price

The bank’s recent activity in the credit markets deserves at least as much attention as the equity milestone. In January, Deutsche Bank issued a €1 billion euro-denominated Tier 2 bond with a maturity of just over 15 years and a first call after roughly 10 years. GlobalCapital described the deal as “landmark”, highlighting that it was among the longest-dated euro Tier 2 instruments issued by a bank since 2020 and that it benefited from strong investor demand for duration in regulatory capital. This was not forced funding. It was opportunistic balance-sheet management at a moment when market access was favorable.

The signal embedded in that transaction is subtle but important. Credit investors were willing to underwrite long-duration subordinated capital because they no longer see Deutsche Bank as a restructuring story with binary risk. Tier 2 buyers are effectively making a judgment about the bank’s ability to remain a going concern well beyond the next cycle. Extending the maturity profile of regulatory capital reduces refinancing risk and smooths the capital stack, something that would have been far harder to achieve when confidence was fragile. In that sense, the deal reinforces the downside protection of the equity.

Capital return becomes the only equity lever

For equity holders, the relevance of the Tier 2 issuance lies in how it interacts with capital return. Deutsche Bank has made clear that it wants to move into a phase of predictable distributions. Management has guided to a return on tangible equity above 10% for 2025 and reiterated a medium-term ambition of more than 13% by 2028. Alongside that, the bank has communicated a payout ratio target of around 60% for the period from 2026 onward, assuming capital levels remain comfortably above regulatory requirements.

At sub-book valuations, buybacks are mechanically attractive. Repurchasing equity below its accounting value boosts book value per share and offers clear accretion even if returns are mediocre. At book value, that mechanical appeal fades. Buybacks still make sense if the bank can earn above its cost of equity, but they no longer generate automatic value. From here, capital return only works if it is durable and underpinned by stable earnings. The equity story shifts from “Can Deutsche Bank return capital?” to “Can it keep doing so through the cycle?”

Structural ROE limits keep the multiple anchored

This is where structural limits assert themselves. Deutsche Bank’s business mix remains capital-intensive, particularly due to its investment banking activities. The investment bank has performed well in recent years, especially in fixed income trading, and has benefited from volatile but active markets. Those revenues can meaningfully lift profits in good periods, but they also raise the volatility of returns and the cost of equity investors apply.

The Financial Times explicitly notes this gap when comparing Deutsche Bank to peers. While analysts expect the bank to hit its 10% return target for 2025, its longer-term ambition of around 13% still lags some European rivals aiming for materially higher returns. A bank earning returns roughly in line with its cost of equity tends to trade around book value. To justify a sustained premium, it needs to demonstrate through-cycle returns that clearly clear that hurdle. In the European regulatory environment, with its emphasis on capital buffers and supervisory caution, that is difficult for an investment-bank-heavy model to achieve.

At roughly €33–34 per share, Deutsche Bank trades close to 1.0x reported and tangible book, implying a market capitalization of about €65 billion. On management’s >10% RoTE target for 2025, the implied earnings yield sits in the high single digits, broadly in line with a 10–11% cost of equity for a European bank with a sizable investment banking arm. Even if the bank reaches its medium-term ambition of ~13% RoTE, a plausible valuation range remains around 1.0–1.2x tangible book, suggesting limited upside from multiple expansion. The share price can grind higher through earnings and distributions, but a meaningful rerating would require returns to exceed the cost of equity by a wider and more durable margin than the current business mix is likely to deliver.

Conclusion

Put together, the equity rerating and the Tier 2 issuance describe a bank that has moved from turnaround to maintenance. The market is no longer debating survival. It is debating consistency. The rerating reflects reduced perceived risk, not the emergence of a new growth engine.

For investors, that means a different risk-reward profile. Deutsche Bank is now priced for execution. Any earnings wobble, regulatory intervention, or capital markets slowdown will feed through quickly because there is little valuation cushion left. Conversely, upside from here depends on management delivering exactly what it has promised: steady returns, tight cost control, and predictable capital distributions, year after year. The recovery phase is over. The maintenance phase has begun.

data.title: World Stocks End 2025 Strong With December Gain
data.description:
As December 2025 came to a close, markets reflected a mixed performance across asset classes. World Stocks led the way, posting a +2.57% return for the month and solidifying their position as 2025’s top performer with an impressive +32.60% gain.

Managed futures finished strong with a +1.15% return in December, marking its fifth consecutive positive month. Despite this late-year rally, Managed Futures ended 2025 nearly flat at -0.19%.

Hedge funds posted a +0.54% gain for December, bringing their year-to-date return to +8.28%. U.S. stocks ended quietly with a +0.08% monthly return, finishing 2025 up +17.71%.

Several asset classes retreated in December. U.S. real estate declined -2.21%, reducing its annual gain to +3.42%. Commodities fell -0.90%, ending the year up +5.92%, while bonds dipped -0.30%, closing 2025 with a +7.10% return.

Cash continued its steady performance with a +0.30% December gain, accumulating a +4.22% return for 2025. The year concluded with all major asset classes except managed futures posting YTD positive returns, led by world stocks’ substantial outperformance.

Past performance is not indicative of future results.

Past performance is not indicative of future results.

Sources: Managed Futures = SocGen CTA Index

Cash = US T-Bill 13 week coupon equivalent annual rate/12, with YTD the sum of each month’s value

Bonds = Vanguard Total Bond Market ETF (BND)

Hedge Funds = IQ Hedge Multi-Strategy Tracker ETF (QAI)

Commodities = iShares S&P GSCI Commodity-Indexed Trust ETF (GSG)

Real Estate = iShares U.S. Real Estate ETF (IYR)

World Stocks = iShares MSCI ACWI ex-U.S. ETF (ACWX)

US Stocks = SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY)

All ETF performance data from Y Charts

Disclaimer

The performance data displayed herein is compiled from various sources, including BarclayHedge, and reports directly from the advisors. These performance figures should not be relied on independent of the individual advisor's disclosure document, which has important information regarding the method of calculation used, whether or not the performance includes proprietary results, and other important footnotes on the advisor's track record.

The programs listed here are a subset of the full list of programs able to be accessed by subscribing to the database and reflect programs we currently work with and/or are more familiar with.

Benchmark index performance is for the constituents of that index only, and does not represent the entire universe of possible investments within that asset class. And further, that there can be limitations and biases to indices such as survivorship, self reporting, and instant history. Individuals cannot invest in the index itself, and actual rates of return may be significantly different and more volatile than those of the index.

Managed futures accounts can subject to substantial charges for management and advisory fees. The numbers within this website include all such fees, but it may be necessary for those accounts that are subject to these charges to make substantial trading profits in the future to avoid depletion or exhaustion of their assets.

Investors interested in investing with a managed futures program (excepting those programs which are offered exclusively to qualified eligible persons as that term is defined by CFTC regulation 4.7) will be required to receive and sign off on a disclosure document in compliance with certain CFT rules The disclosure documents contains a complete description of the principal risk factors and each fee to be charged to your account by the CTA, as well as the composite performance of accounts under the CTA's management over at least the most recent five years. Investor interested in investing in any of the programs on this website are urged to carefully read these disclosure documents, including, but not limited to the performance information, before investing in any such programs.

Those investors who are qualified eligible persons as that term is defined by CFTC regulation 4.7 and interested in investing in a program exempt from having to provide a disclosure document and considered by the regulations to be sophisticated enough to understand the risks and be able to interpret the accuracy and completeness of any performance information on their own.

RCM receives a portion of the commodity brokerage commissions you pay in connection with your futures trading and/or a portion of the interest income (if any) earned on an account's assets. The listed manager may also pay RCM a portion of the fees they receive from accounts introduced to them by RCM.

Limitations on RCM Quintile + Star Rankings

The Quintile Rankings and RCM Star Rankings shown here are provided for informational purposes only. RCM does not guarantee the accuracy, timeliness or completeness of this information. The ranking methodology is proprietary and the results have not been audited or verified by an independent third party. Some CTAs may employ trading programs or strategies that are riskier than others. CTAs may manage customer accounts differently than their model results shown or make different trades in actual customer accounts versus their own accounts. Different CTAs are subject to different market conditions and risks that can significantly impact actual results. RCM and its affiliates receive compensation from some of the rated CTAs. Investors should perform their own due diligence before investing with any CTA. This ranking information should not be the sole basis for any investment decision.

See the full terms of use and risk disclaimer here.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
